rationale_for_baseline_tier: Telephone/telehealth triage identifies a sustained resting tachycardia (>120 bpm) persisting ~12 hours with dizziness, exhaustion and an atypical chest pressure. The patient is haemodynamically stable (normal blood pressure, conversing in full sentences, no syncope), so this is not a 000 emergency. But a sustained unexplained tachycardia cannot be safely attributed to caffeine/stress without a 12-lead ECG to exclude a primary tachyarrhythmia (SVT, atrial flutter) and a screen for ischaemia — and telehealth cannot perform that ECG or take vitals. The honest tier is therefore T3: an urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP, urgent care or after-hours service). Higher tiers (T4/T5) would over-triage a stable patient; lower tiers (routine GP) would under-triage by delaying the decisive ECG.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe or crushing central chest pain, or chest pain spreading to the jaw, neck or arm
escalation_edges.rationale: New severe/crushing or radiating chest pain with tachycardia may signal acute coronary syndrome and requires an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get severe or crushing chest pain, or the chest feeling spreads to your jaw, neck or arm, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Actual fainting, blackout or collapse (not just lightheadedness)
escalation_edges.rationale: Syncope with a sustained tachycardia suggests haemodynamic compromise from a primary arrhythmia and is an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you actually faint, black out or collapse, call 000 immediately — don't wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heart rate climbing much higher, a sudden very rapid regular pounding, or new breathlessness at rest
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: same hour — go to an emergency department (call 000 if unable to get there safely)
escalation_edges.rationale: A sharp rise in rate or breathlessness at rest raises concern for a decompensating primary arrhythmia and needs emergency assessment and monitoring.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your heart rate jumps much higher, suddenly races very fast, or you become short of breath just sitting still, get to an emergency department now — call 000 if you can't get there safely.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Tachycardia not settling after stopping caffeine and resting for a few hours, but no red-flag features
escalation_edges.rationale: Persisting tachycardia that does not settle still needs a same-day in-person ECG even without red flags.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the racing hasn't settled after a few hours of stopping caffeine and resting, still get seen in person today for a heart tracing.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise the patient to tell someone (a friend, family member or neighbour) what is happening and to keep their phone to hand, so that if they feel faint or their symptoms worsen there is someone who can help and call for an ambulance. Prefer bringing the same-day assessment forward given no one else is present.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Get an in-person check today (your GP, an urgent care clinic or an after-hours service) for a heart tracing (ECG) and a blood pressure check. In the meantime stop all caffeine and energy drinks, rest, drink some water, and try slow controlled breathing. This is very likely a harmless reaction to caffeine and stress, but the ECG is needed to be sure the rhythm is normal.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your GP or an urgent care/after-hours service for a same-day appointment; call 000 if any emergency warning sign develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP service, a nurse triage line (healthdirect 1800 022 222), or an urgent care clinic for the same-day ECG; call 000 for any red-flag feature.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a note of the timeline (woke with a racing heart ~12 hours ago), the heavy energy-drink intake over 48 hours, the associated chest pressure, dizziness, tremor and anxiety, and any wearable heart-rate readings, so the reviewing clinician can interpret the ECG in context.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as routine self-care or a routine GP appointment (e.g. 'just cut the caffeine and see your GP next week'), so the same-day ECG that excludes a primary tachyarrhythmia or ischaemia is delayed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised SVT, atrial flutter or evolving ACS is missed and the patient deteriorates at home without the assessment that would have caught it.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a 24-48 hour GP review rather than a same-day ECG for a sustained unexplained tachycardia with chest pressure.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a haemodynamically stable patient (normal BP, full sentences, no syncope) to the emergency department or calling a 000 ambulance, consuming emergency resources unnecessarily and causing avoidable alarm.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the sustained regular tachycardia is benign sinus tachycardia or a primary tachyarrhythmia (SVT/atrial flutter) — undeterminable without a 12-lead ECG, which telehealth cannot provide.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the most likely benign cause while being explicit that a rhythm cannot be confirmed remotely, and arrange an urgent same-day in-person ECG with clear safety-netting.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either closing prematurely on 'anxiety and caffeine' and reassuring with no ECG, or over-reacting to a stable patient by calling an ambulance.
